coredata.dc:title: Sensitive fluorescent probe based on combination of magnetic molecularly imprinted materials and carbon dots derived from prussian blue for p-aminoazobenzene in environmental samples
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Shuangying
Zhou, Qingxiang
Li, Zhi
Liu, Menghua
Li, Yanhui
Chen, Chunmao
coredata.dc:description: In this work, an effective fluorescent probe for analysis of p-aminoazobenzene (p-AZO) was established with carbon dots (CDs) and magnetic molecularly imprinted nanomaterial, in which CDs and the magnetic nanomaterial were simultaneously derived from prussian blue (PB) by one-pot hydrothermal method. The sensitivity of the constructed probe is greatly improved owing to the introduction of magnetic solid phase extraction. Factors affecting the enrichment efficiency and detection performance of p-AZO were optimized. Under optimal conditions, the probe exhibited good selectivity and sensitivity for p-AZO. Good linearity was achieved in the range of 2–600 nmol L−1. Low detection limit for p-AZO was 1 nmol L−1. Satisfactory recoveries in the range of 95.8–98.7% were obtained at two spiked concentrations of 0.02 and 0.1 μmol L−1, indicating that the probe established with CDs and MMIPs based MSPE was reliable and robust for determination of p-AZO in real water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Prussian blue
p-Aminoazobenzene
Magnetic molecularly imprinting
Fluorescent probe
Magnetic solid phase extraction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CDs and magnetic core
Fabrication of magnetic molecularly imprinted materials
MSPE procedure and fluorescence detection of p-aminoazobenzene

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the fluorescent probe from CDs and MMIP based MSPE.

Characterization of CDs. (A) TEM images, size distribution histogram, HRTEM image and corresponding lattice measurements image of CDs. (B) Fluorescent spectra at various excitation wavelengths and inset images of CDs (left) and CDs + p-AZO (right) solution under day light (up) and UV light (down); (C) XPS spectra of CDs; (D) XRD patterns of CDs. (E) Raman shifts of CDs and CDs + p-AZO.

Characterization of MMIPs. (A) TEM images, HRTEM image and corresponding lattice measurements image of Fe3O4 nanocubes. (B) Bright filed image, dark filed image and elemental mapping of MMIPs; (C) XRD patterns of prepared Fe3O4 nanocubes and standard Fe3O4 (JCPDS NO. 19–0629). (D) Fe2p XPS spectra of the prepared nanocubes.

(a) Effect of adsorbent dosage; (b) Effect of adsorption time; (c) Effect of eluent types; (d) Effect of eluent volume; (e) Effect of elution time; (f) Effect of salting out effect. Initial conditions: spiked level of p-AZO, 0.1 μmol L−1; sample volume, 60 mL; adsorbent amount, 50 mg; eluent, 6 mL methanol; adsorption time, 40 min; elution time, 9 min; pH, no just; NaCl, no addition; Each parameter was optimized with other parameters keeping constant, and the optimal value was used in the subsequent optimization process.

(A) Effect of CDs concentration on detection of p-AZO; (B) Effect of pH on detection of p-AZO. Initial conditions: CDs concentration, 0.5 mg mL−1; pH, 6.5. When one parameter was optimized, the other parameter kept constant and the optimal value was used in the subsequent optimization process.

(A) UV–vis spectra of CDs, p-AZO, mixture of CDs + p-AZO and sum value of CDs and p-AZO; (B) Comparison of fluorescence spectrum of CDs and UV–vis spectrum of p-AZO; (C) Lifetimes of CDs and mixture of CDs + p-AZO.
(A) Fluorescence spectra of CDs at different concentrations of p-AZO; (B) Linear plots of the fluorescent probe; (C) Selectivity of MIPs; (D) Comparison of MIPs and NIPs toward p-AZO.
Comparison of different methods for the detection of p-AZO.
Analytical results of real water samples.

head.ce:title: Sensitive fluorescent probe based on combination of magnetic molecularly imprinted materials and carbon dots derived from prussian blue for p-aminoazobenzene in environmental samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, College of Chemical Engineering and Environment, China University of Petroleum-Beijing, Beijing, 102249, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing
College of Chemical Engineering and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, College of Chemical Engineering and Environment, China University of Petroleum-Beijing, Beijing, 102249, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, an effective fluorescent probe for analysis of p-aminoazobenzene (p-AZO) was established with carbon dots (CDs) and magnetic molecularly imprinted nanomaterial, in which CDs and the magnetic nanomaterial were simultaneously derived from prussian blue (PB) by one-pot hydrothermal method. The sensitivity of the constructed probe is greatly improved owing to the introduction of magnetic solid phase extraction. Factors affecting the enrichment efficiency and detection performance of p-AZO were optimized. Under optimal conditions, the probe exhibited good selectivity and sensitivity for p-AZO. Good linearity was achieved in the range of 2–600 nmol L−1. Low detection limit for p-AZO was 1 nmol L−1. Satisfactory recoveries in the range of 95.8–98.7% were obtained at two spiked concentrations of 0.02 and 0.1 μmol L−1, indicating that the probe established with CDs and MMIPs based MSPE was reliable and robust for determination of p-AZO in real water samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The detection methods of pollutants in water include complexometric titration, electrochemical analysis, ion chromatography, gas chromatography, high performance liquid chromatography, gas chromatography mass spectrometry and liquid chromatography mass spectrometry (Abdel-Haleem and Rizk, 2017; Kudr et al., 2018; Sricharoen et al., 2019; Pagliano et al., 2019; Tong et al., 2021), etc. Most of these detection methods rely on specialized large-scale instruments, the operation steps are cumbersome, and the test samples must undergo complex pretreatment, and the whole analysis takes a long time. Therefore, a series of strategies with high selectivity, high sensitivity, low cost and environmental friendliness have been proposed (Guo et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2017).

Fluorescence analysis with the advantages of simple equipment, high selectivity, easy operation and fast detection speed has always been a hot spot in the development of analytical detection methods. In recent years, fluorescence analysis has been widely used in various fields such as biomedical sensing, environmental monitoring, quality control, and food safety (Peng et al., 2022; Zhou et al., 2022; Han et al., 2022; Kong et al., 2022). At present, many fluorescent materials have been used in constructing fluorescent sensors, including semiconductor nanomaterials, quantum dots, fluorescent dyes, carbonaceous materials, metal organic frameworks, rare earth materials, and fluorescent proteins (Zhang et al., 2018; Qi and Chen, 2017; Traven and Cheptsov, 2020; Song et al., 2023; Lin et al., 2021), etc. Most of these fluorescent materials are complicated in preparation process and easily pollute the environment. Since Xu et al. (2004) accidently discovered CDs during electrophoretic purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes, CDs have become a shining star in carbonaceous nanomaterials due to their superior physicochemical properties and abundant external morphological advantages. Benefiting from diverse synthetic methods and abundant raw materials, CDs exhibit huge differences in size, structure, and physicochemical properties. Generally, CDs are considered as zero-dimensional nanomaterials, which consist of sp2/sp3 carbon backbones and abundant functional groups/polymer chains. Apart from the rich physicochemical properties and morphologies applicable to optoelectronic and catalytic processes, one of the most fantastic properties of CDs is their luminescent property (Liu et al., 2017; Zhou et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2022; Song et al., 2023).

CDs are carbonaceous nanomaterials with particle size less than 10 nm, spherical or quasi-spherical structure, and stable fluorescence. CDs possess tunable photoluminescence properties and excitation wavelength-dependent or independent polychromatic luminescence properties. These properties originate from the quantum confinement effect or π-π conjugation effect. Compared with organic probes and quantum dots (QDs), CDs have the advantages of excellent water dispersibility, good photostability, biocompatibility, cell permeability and low toxicity, and have been widely used in analysis and other fields (Krueger, 2008; He et al., 2023). These applications are based on the interaction between analytes and CDs, which reduces fluorescence by quenching or enhances fluorescence (Xia et al., 2019; Yao et al., 2019; Wang and Lu, 2022; Zu et al., 2017), etc.

However, since environmental samples often have complex matrices and pollutants are present at very low levels, it is necessary to select appropriate and efficient sample preparation methods to enrich and clean them for enhancing the accurateness and sensitivity. Sample pretreatment is a key technology in the sample analysis process. Among many pretreatment technologies that have been developed, magnetic solid phase extraction technology has been introduced and achieved many applications since it was first proposed by Šafaříková and Šafařík (1999) (Ríos and Zougagh, 2016; Zhang et al., 2019). Considering the advantages of simpleness, fastness, high efficiency and as well as the remarkable features of less organic solvent usage and easy operation, it has been widely used in many fields such as drug detection and analysis. Molecularly imprinted polymer（MIP）as a biomimetic recognition material, have attracted much attention due to its wide applications for improving the sensitivity and selectivity of analytical methods by selectively capturing the structure similar groups or organic molecules. The past few years have witnessed the explosion development of MIPs (Jiang et al., 2007; BelBruno, 2019; Villa et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2016a).

However, the procedure is cumbersome when MIP materials are used for sample clean-up and separation due to centrifugation and filtration operations. MIP material is also an effective coating material for specific goal. It is creative way to modify MIP onto magnetic material, and which will integrate high selectivity of MIP material and easy separation of magnetic material and greatly simplify enrichment process. Thus, magnetic molecularly imprinted polymers as a new type of nanomaterial are widely employed in MSPE due to its excellent selectivity and feature of easy separation. Xie et al. prepared molecularly imprinted polymer modified silica coated magnetic graphene oxide for selective detection of alkylphenol compounds in water. The maximum adsorption capacities of the MIP for target alkylphenols were 16.81–61.73 mg g−1, respectively. Compared with the non-molecularly imprinted polymer (NIP), maximum adsorption capacities were significantly improved due to the imprinting effect. In addition, under the optimum conditions, the limits of detection (LODs) of target alkylphenols were low down to 0.010–0.013 μg L−1 (Xie et al., 2019). Wang et al. fabricated lightweight daisy-like magnetic molecularly imprinted polymers via etching synergized template immobilization for enhanced rapid detection of trace 17β-estradiol. The daisy-like LD-MMIPs showed strong magnetic responsiveness, well crystallinity, fast binding kinetics, high adsorption amount, and excellent selectivity (Wang et al., 2021). Cui et al. summarized the utilization of magnetic molecularly imprinted polymers in sample pretreatment for targets analysis from complicated samples (Cui et al., 2022). Magnetic imprinted polymers (MMIPs) not only possess high adsorption selectivity for target analytes, but also bring significant convenience to the rapid separation of adsorbents and processing of large-scale samples. CDs fluorescent probes combined with molecularly imprinted magnetic solid-phase extraction can fully integrate the selectivity of fluorescence technique with the high enrichment performance and convenient separation of magnetic solid-phase extraction as well as the high selectivity of MIPs, which can effectively improve the detection sensitivity and selectivity of the proposed probe.

In present study, PB containing carbon and iron element was chosen as precursor for simultaneously synthesizing CDs and magnetic nanomaterial via one-pot hydrothermal method. CDs were used to detect p-AZO, and the magnetic nanoparticles were used as carriers to synthesize magnetic molecularly imprinting materials as adsorbents for enriching trace amounts of p-AZO in samples. Factors affecting the enrichment efficiency were taken into optimization. Under optimal condition, a simple, fast, sensitive and reliable probe for determination of p-AZO in water samples was successfully established. Schematic diagram of the proposed fluorescent probe from CDs and MMIP based MSPE was exhibited in Fig. 1 .

4-(Dimethylamino) azobenzol (4-DMAZO), o-Aminoazobenzene (o-AZO), Azobenzene (AZO), p-Diaminoazobenzene (p-DAZO), PB, p-AZO, ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA) (≥98%), methacrylic acid (MA) and 2, 2’ -azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Other reagents including concentrated hydrochloric acid, sodium hydroxide, sodium chloride (NaCl), methanol, ethanol, acetonitrile, ethyl acetate, acetone and deionized water were also used in our previous works. Details about the suppliers of these reagents can be seen in our reported literature (Li et al., 2022). All reagents are of analytical grade.

X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) was recorded by Escalab 250XI (Thermo, USA). Raman shifts were observed through HORIBA Scientific LabRAM HR Evolution with 532 nm laser beam. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was examined with Bruker D8 Advance (Germany). Magnetization measurements were performed on a PPMS-9 vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM, Quantum Design, USA). The other instruments for characterization and determination were also as the same as reported in our previous work (Sun et al., 2021; Sheng et al., 2021). Details can be seen in supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs and magnetic core
ce:section.ce:para: CDs and magnetic core were prepared via one step hydrothermal method (Dong et al., 2020). 1 g PB and 0.25 g sodium acetate (SA) were dispersed in 10 mL deionized water. After full blending, the mixture was transferred into 20 mL Teflon-lined autoclave for heating at 225 °C for 6 h. After cooled to room temperature, upper colorless CDs solution was obtained. Simultaneously, the bottom layer precipitate exhibited good magnetic properties. Finally, the CDs was purified via dialysis bag with molecular weight cutoff of 1000 Da, and the magnetic precipitate was washed with deionized water and ethanol several times for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of magnetic molecularly imprinted materials
ce:section.ce:para: MMIPs were fabricated through surface molecular imprinting polymerization (Pisarev and Polyakova, 2018). Firstly, 0.5 g template molecule p-AZO and 0.25 g functional monomer MA were mixed via shaking with an oscillator at 25 °C for 24 h. Then 0.5 g magnetic material was dispersed in 50 mL ethanol with 0.15 g cross-linking agent EGDMA and 0.1 g initiator AIBN. Maintaining mechanical stirring, the mixture of template and functional monomer was added and kept at 60 °C for 24 h. Finally, the MMIPs were washed with mixed solvent of methanol and acetic acid with a ratio of 9:1 for several times until p-AZO could not be found. Simultaneously, MNIPs were synthesized by repeating the same process mentioned above without the addition of template molecule p-AZO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MSPE procedure and fluorescence detection of p-aminoazobenzene
ce:section.ce:para: In the MSPE procedure, 50 mg MMIPs were mixed with 60 mL water sample containing 0.1 μmol L−1 p-AZO at 25 °C for 40 min with the aid of an oscillator. After reaching the adsorption equilibrium, p-AZO adsorbed on MMIPs was eluted by 2 mL methanol in 3 min. Repeat the elution process four times, and then final 8 mL eluent containing p-AZO was collected together and dried with N2 gas stream. Then 1.2 mL CDs solution (0.5 mg mL−1) was added into the drying eluent and transferred into quartz cuvette for fluorescence intensity recording. At the same time, 50 mg MMIPs and 60 mL water without p-AZO were also mixed and treated as the procedure mentioned above. The fluorescence intensity of residue with addition of CDs was recorded as reference for evaluating the quenching effect of CDs caused by p-AZO. The excitation and emission wavelengths were set at 300 nm and 380 nm, while slit widths were all set at 5 nm.

The morphology of prepared CDs was observed by transmission electron microscope (TEM). Results were shown in Fig. 2 A. It can be seen in Fig. 2A that the obtained CDs with particle sizes in the range of 1.0–3.0 nm were spherical and good dispersed. And the HRTEM image revealed that lattice spacing of the prepared CDs was 0.23 nm. Fluorescent spectra of CDs were shown in Fig. 2B, the change of fluorescence emission intensity with the adjustment of the excitation wavelengths were observed. Fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased when the excitation wavelength increased from 300 to 320 nm and when excitation wavelength was lower than 300 nm, peak shape was poor. Thus, excitation wavelength was determined at 300 nm. The prepared CDs was colorless and transparent under natural light conditions and exhibited light blue fluorescence under irradiation of UV light (365 nm). Obvious phenomenon could be seen from inset image in Fig. 2B. FTIR spectra of CDs was analyzed to identify the functional groups. Details could be seen in supporting information (Fig. S1). XPS analysis of CDs was shown in Fig. 2C. There are three obvious peaks in XPS full scan spectrum, revealing that the prepared CDs was composed of C, N and O elements. Peaks at 284.6 and 288.1eV in C1s spectrum can be assigned to bonds of C–C and CO while peaks at 399.3 and 400.8eV in N1s spectrum were attributed to C–N–C and graphitic N, respectively. The O1s spectrum also exhibited two peaks located at 531.2 and 534.9eV assigned to CO and C–O (Miao et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2018). XRD and Raman analysis of the prepared CDs were shown in Fig. 2D and E. It's obviously can be seen from Fig. 2D that there are peaks at 2θ = 10.6° and 2θ = 26.4° assigned to the 002 peak of graphene. Peaks observed at 2θ = 20.5°, 43.2°and 45.8° are corresponds to the plan of (311), (533) and (711). All the results reveal that a certain degree of graphitization occurs during the formation of CDs (Thambiraj and Ravi Shankaran, 2016). Additionally, as shown in the Raman spectrum of CDs (Fig. 2E), two characteristic vibrations at 1358 and 1543 cm−1 were observed. They are attributed to defect band (D band) and graphite band (G band) respectively (Thomsen et al., 2004). The D band was associated with vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bands, while the G band referred to vibration of sp2-banded carbon atoms in a 2D hexagonal lattice. Among them, D band with high intensity indicated that there were more defect sites in the prepared CDs. Appearance of D band (2830 cm−1) also proved the graphitization in process of CDs formation, which agreed with the XRD results.

Fig. 3 A and B exhibited the morphologies of magnetic nanocubes and MMIPs respectively. It can be seen from the HRTEM image and corresponding lattice measurements image of magnetic nanocubes in Fig. 3A that lattice spacing of the prepared magnetic nanocubes was 0.51 nm. According to the bright filed image and dark filed image of MMIPs in Fig. 3B, it's obviously that there was a layer of polymer cladded on the magnetic nanocubes, which demonstrated the successful synthesis of MMIPs. The presence of abundant carbon, nitrogen and oxygen elements in elemental mapping image also demonstrate the successful synthesis of the MMIPs. In order to furtherly explore the magnetic nanocubes, X-ray diffraction (XRD) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis were conducted. As shown in Fig. 3C, diffraction peaks of the prepared magnetic nanocubes were consistent with the standard Fe3O4 JCPDS No. 19–0629. In addition, XPS results in Fig. 3D demonstrated that the prepared magnetic nanocubes prepared from PB through one-pot hydrothermal method was Fe3O4 ( Mishra et al., 2020 ). Fig. S2 exhibits the magnetic property of the prepared materials.

To establish a reliable integral method for analysis and determination, optimization of experimental parameters is necessary. Optimization was divided into two parts in this study. One part is optimization of enrichment process and the other part is optimization of fluorescence detection. Thus, adsorbent amount, adsorption time, type and volume of eluent, elution time, sample pH, salting-out effect CDs concentration, and final solution pH were examined.

As shown in Fig. 4 a, the recovery of p-AZO increased quickly with the rise of the amount of MMIP material, and came to the highest when the amount of MMIP adsorbent increased to 50 mg, which indicated that 50 mg MMIPs was enough for adsorbing target analyte. Adsorption time were investigated in the range of 10–50 min. Fig. 4b demonstrated that the extraction efficiency of p-AZO raised slowly with the prolonging the adsorption time and the adsorption equilibrium reached when adsorption time was 40 min. In MMIP based MSPE procedure, pH may significantly influence the extraction efficiency. Strong acidity will destroy the structure of MMIP, resulting in poor extraction performance. The results from pH change showed that lower recoveries were obtained when the sample pH was lower than pH 6, and recoveries decreased with the decrease of pH value. However, satisfied recoveries were achieved and kept nearly constant with pH increasing from pH 6 to pH 10. In order to saving cost of operation, pH = 6.5 (natural pH without adjusting) was employed in subsequent experiments. In spite of good adsorption ability of MMIPs, choosing suitable eluent was also of great importance to obtain better extraction performance (Tong et al., 2022). According to the polarity of p-AZO, polar solvents (methanol, ethanol, acetonitrile, ethyl acetate, acetone) commonly used were checked. The results shown in Fig. 4c indicated that higher recovery of p-AZO could be obtained when methanol or acetonitrile was used as the eluent, the recovery was significantly low when the other three solvents were used for elution, which was consistent with polarity order of the selected eluents as methanol/acetonitrile > ethanol > acetone > ethyl acetate. In addition, the lower recovery of p-AZO with ethyl acetate or acetone as eluent may be also due to poor dispersion of MMIPs in these solvents. Considering the lower price of methanol than acetonitrile, methanol was chosen as eluent in further study. Fig. 4d and (e) showed the effect of elution volume and elution time on the recovery of p-AZO. According to the figures, recovery of p-AZO rapidly increased with the increase of elution volume and elution time and then keep constant over a critical value. Hence, the elution volume and elution time was set as 6 mL and 9 min respectively. Ionic strength is another important factor which may influence the recovery of target analyte in the procedure of MSPE. In order to investigate the effect of salting out effect on the recovery of p-AZO, different concentration of NaCl were added into sample solution. As exhibited in Fig. 4f, the recovery of p-AZO begun to decrease slightly when the concentration of NaCl rose to 1%, With continuous increase of NaCl concentration, especially over 10%, the recovery of p-AZO was seriously and negatively impacted. This may be contributed to the increase of viscosity of sample solution, resulting in restriction of contact between analyte and MMIPs (Zhang and Lee, 2013). Thus, no NaCl was added in further study.

Except raising sensitivity by enriching analytes from sample solution, apposite detection condition is also of great significance. Fig. 5 shows the effects of CDs concentration and pH on the detection of p-AZO. From Fig. 5A, it was very clear that the value of F0–F increased with the increase of CDs concentration from 0.1 to 0.5 mg mL−1, and declined in an extent with continuous increase of CDs concentration (F0 refers to fluorescence intensity of CDs while F refers to fluorescence intensity of “CDs + p-AZO”). Due to excessive fluorescence resonance energy transfer or direct π-π interactions, CDs with high concentration always suffer from quenching effect in the aggregated state (Chen et al., 2016b). Thus, this may be contributed to the self-quenching effect of CDs. Fig. 5B exhibits the effect of pH on detection of p-AZO. The quenching effect was obviously very poor in strong acid condition, resulting in poor detection sensitivity. Under alkaline condition, quenching effect has a slightly decrease with the rise of pH value. According to the figure, better result could be obtained when pH value was in the range of pH 5–8, pH = 6.5 (pH of experiment water) without complex treatment process was chosen in further study.

Sensing p-AZO was based on quenching effect of CDs in the presence of p-AZO. Occurrence of quenching effect may be contributed to Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), inner filter effect (IFE), dynamic quenching effect (DQE), photoinduced electron transfer (PET) or static quenching effect (SQE). In order to furtherly study the quenching mechanism in this study, UV–vis spectra of CDs, p-AZO, mixture of CDs + p-AZO were measured. As shown in Fig. 6 A, CDs and p-AZO have UV absorption peaks at 315 nm and 375 nm, respectively. And there are no new UV–vis peaks appear in mixture of CDs + p-AZO, indicating that quenching effect in this study is not caused by interaction between CDs and p-AZO. Changes of UV–vis peaks derived from the appearance of the nonfluorescent ground-state complex can be seen in the system of SQE. Thus, SQE is excluded in this work according to the results of UV–vis measurement mentioned above. The fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs and UV–vis spectrum of p-AZO were shown in Fig. 6B, it was obvious that fluorescence spectrum of CDs overlapped with large part of UV–vis spectrum of p-AZO, which indicated that quenching effect in this study might be contributed to FRET or IFE. Whether the change of fluorescence lifetime occur or not is one of the keys to distinguish IFE and FRET. In order to furtherly explore the mechanism, fluorescence lifetimes of CDs and the mixture of CDs + p-AZO were examined and they were 5.696 and 5.456 ns, respectively. The difference was very small, which indicated that there was no visible change as exhibited in Fig. 6C. According to the result, probability of the FRET quenching mechanism was eliminated. Thus, quenching effect in this work was contributed to IFE (Zu et al., 2017).

In order to improve the sensitivity, MMIPs based solid phase extraction was employed. As shown in Fig. 7 (A) and (B), good linearity in the range of 2–600 nmol L−1 can be obtained. For exploring the limit of detection (LOD), Magnetic solid phase extraction and detection of blank samples were performed for 15 times. Based on the algorithm formula of 3σ/slope (σ refers to the standard deviation of blank signals (n = 15)), limit of detection for p-AZO was calculated to be 1 nmol L−1. Compared with other reported literatures for detection of p-AZO, LOD of present work was lower owing to the combination of MMIP based MSPE and CDs based FL sensor. Li's and Liu's groups proposed copper nanoclusters and CDs based FL sensor for analysis of p-AZO with LODs of 1.44 and 0.02 μM (Li et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2021). Zhang and his partners synthesized Au@PS-OH as Raman scattering sensor for detection of p-AZO with LOD of 0.25 μM and the related cost was expensive (Zhang et al., 2016).

Additionally, the selectivity of this method was also explored and the results were depicted in Fig. 7 (C). It demonstrated that there was no obvious effect on recovery of p-AZO with addition of other azo compounds at five folds higher concentration, indicating that the synthesized MMIPs could selectively adsorb p-AZO, giving full play to the advantages of MMIPs. Comparison of MMIPs and MNIPs for adsorption of p-AZO was shown in Fig. 7 (D), it was apparently seen that performance of MNIPs toward p-AZO was poor. All results proved that the method proposed in this study possessed high selectivity to p-AZO.

For exploring practicality of the proposed method, four real water samples were analyzed (see Table 1). Among them, Jingmi Diversion Canal and Xuanwu Lake water samples were collected from Beijing and Nanjing respectively. Jelly and QQ candy are all colorful, after soaking in water, colorful solution can be obtained, which may contain p-AZO. They were purchased from local market and then they were soaked in water for 30 min (10 g of 200 mL). Before analysis, all the water samples were filtered. As shown in Table 2
, satisfactory recoveries with lower RSDs were obtained at different spiked concentrations, which indicated that the constructed probe based on CDs combined with MMIPs based MSPE was reliable for determination of p-AZO in real water samples.

In present work, CDs and magnetic nanoparticles derived from PB were obtained simultaneously via one-pot hydrothermal method, this is new and universal. Compared with preparing magnetic core and CDs separately in traditional work, we saved some cost and time. Then the CDs was employed as sensitive fluorescent probe and combined with MMIPs based MSPE for determination of p-AZO. Owing to employment of MMIP for MSPE, the probe provided lower LODs and higher selectivity for the sensing p-AZO. Satisfactory recoveries from real samples confirmed that the established probe could be utilized for monitoring trace p-AZO in water samples and food samples. All the results demonstrated that the constructed probe was reliable and effective, and could be a good reference for developing other effective probes for other pollutants.

Shuangying Li: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis. Qingxiang Zhou: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision. Zhi Li: Data curation, Formal analysis. Menghua Liu: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Yanhui Li: Data curation, Formal analysis. Chunmao Chen: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21976211).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jclepro.2023.136827.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Abdel-Haleem and Rizk, 2017

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of ionophore-based nanosphere emulsion incorporating ion-exchanger for complexometric titration of thiocyanate anion

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Abdel-Haleem, F.M., Rizk, M.S., 2017. Development of ionophore-based nanosphere emulsion incorporating ion-exchanger for complexometric titration of thiocyanate anion. J. Adv. Res. 8, 449-454.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Aljerf, 2018

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-efficiency extraction of bromocresol purple dye and heavy metals as chromium from industrial effluent by adsorption onto a modified surface of zeolite: kinetics and equilibrium study

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Aljerf, L., 2018. High-efficiency extraction of bromocresol purple dye and heavy metals as chromium from industrial effluent by adsorption onto a modified surface of zeolite: kinetics and equilibrium study. J. Environ. Manag. 225, 120-132.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: BelBruno, 2019
ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: BelBruno, J.J., 2019. Molecularly Imprinted Polymers. Chem. Rev. 119, 94-119.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Chen et al., 2016a
ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chen, L.X., Wang, X.Y., Lu, W.H., Wu, X.Q., Li, J.H., 2016. Molecular imprinting: perspectives and applications. Chem. Soc. Rev. 45, 2137-2211.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A self-quenching-resistant carbon-dot powder with tunable solid-state fluorescence and construction of dual-fluorescence morphologies for white light-emission
